content:
Real CI minutes, a lockfile surprise that cost us an afternoon, and the one service where we kept pip anyway.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Python dependency tooling has a new default in 2026, and most of the praise for it is vague. Ours won’t be. Numbers below, not vibes.
We run a monorepo — 40 internal packages, shared via a workspace layout, built and tested on every PR. Poetry had managed it for three years. It worked. It was also slow enough that nobody questioned the coffee-break-length CI runs until someone finally timed one.
The baseline nobody had measured
Before touching anything, we pulled three weeks of CI history for the full test-and-lint pipeline:
→ Cold dependency install (poetry install, no cache): 4m 40s average → Warm cache hit: 1m 55s → Cache miss rate on our CI runners: ~35% — Poetry's cache keying on our monorepo's shared lockfile invalidated more often than it should have, because one package's version bump busted the cache for all 40 → Full pipeline (install + test + lint) across our busiest day: installs alone consumed roughly 3.1 CI-hours cluster-wide
That last number is what got budget attention. Not “CI feels slow.” A dollar figure attached to install time specifically.
The migration — what actually changed
uv replaced poetry install, poetry add, poetry lock, and our virtualenv bootstrapping. It did not replace our pyproject.toml structure — that stayed, since uv reads standard [project] metadata and doesn't force Poetry's [tool.poetry] dialect rewrite if you're already close to PEP 621.
uv venv
uv pip install -r requirements.txt # first pass, pre-lockfile
uv sync # once pyproject.toml + uv.lock existedCold install after migration: 11 seconds. Not a typo. Warm: 1.4 seconds. The 4m40s → 11s number is the one everyone quotes, and it’s real, but it’s also the easy part — uv is a Rust binary with a global cache and parallel resolution, it's supposed to be fast at this.
The part nobody quotes: cache hit rate on CI went from ~65% to 97% because uv's dependency resolution is deterministic in a way that doesn't cascade-invalidate across unrelated packages in the workspace the way our Poetry setup did. That's the number that actually moved our CI-hours bill.
The lockfile surprise that cost an afternoon
uv.lock and poetry.lock are not interchangeable, and the transition period is where migrations go sideways.
We generated uv.lock fresh from pyproject.toml rather than trying to convert poetry.lock. Sounds clean. It picked slightly different resolved versions for three transitive dependencies — nothing our direct pyproject.toml constraints disagreed with, all technically valid resolutions, all newer patch versions than what Poetry had pinned for over a year.
One of those three was a transitive pin on cryptography. The newer patch version changed a default cipher parameter in a way that broke one internal service's integration test — not a bug, a behavior change we'd been silently protected from by an old lock.
uv pip install cryptography==<old-pinned-version>
uv lock --upgrade-package cryptographyLesson: a lockfile isn’t just about speed, it’s an implicit contract about which versions you’ve actually tested. Regenerating it from scratch throws that contract away and replaces it with “whatever resolves today.” Do it deliberately, one workspace at a time, and run full integration suites — not just unit tests — against the new lock before merging.
We lost about 3 hours to that one dependency. Cheap, in hindsight, against multiple CI-hours saved per week. Still — budget for it. Nobody warned us and we didn’t warn ourselves.
Where we deliberately did not migrate
One service in the monorepo stayed on plain pip with a hand-pinned requirements.txt. It's a legacy data pipeline, three people ever touch it, and its dependency tree includes two internal packages installed via git+ssh:// URLs with commit-pin dependencies that predate a clean packaging structure.
uv handles git dependencies fine. The problem wasn't uv — it was that converting that one package's ad hoc structure into something uv sync could manage cleanly was a half-day job for a service that runs twice a month and has never once been CI-bottlenecked. The install time there is 40 seconds either way, run four times a month. Migrating it was pure yak-shaving with no measurable return.
The rule we landed on: migrate where install time × run frequency is large enough to show up in a CI bill. Skip where it isn’t, no matter how tidy uv-everywhere would look in the retro slide.
The other surprise: editable installs and the workspace layout
Poetry’s path-dependency handling for our internal packages ({ path = "../shared-auth", develop = true }) had a direct analog in uv's workspace support, but the migration wasn't a find-and-replace. uv wants a [tool.uv.workspace] table at the repo root declaring member paths explicitly:
[tool.uv.workspace]
members = ["packages/*"][tool.uv.sources]
shared-auth = { workspace = true }We’d assumed glob members would just work the way Poetry’s path dependencies had. First run flagged six packages whose directory names didn’t match their PyPI-style import names — a mismatch Poetry had silently tolerated because path dependencies don’t care what the folder is called, only what’s in pyproject.toml. uv's workspace resolution is stricter about this, and stricter turned out to be right — those six were a genuine source of confusion for anyone new to the repo, we just hadn't been forced to fix it before.
Renaming six directories and updating their imports took another afternoon, unrelated to uv per se, just uncovered by it. Add that to the migration cost column if you're estimating your own timeline — the tool doesn't just migrate your lockfile, it audits your repo structure whether you asked it to or not.
The CI config itself, before and after
The actual pipeline YAML change was smaller than either the lockfile story or the workspace story suggests:
# before
- run: pip install poetry==1.8.3
- run: poetry install --no-interaction --no-root# after
- run: curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
- run: uv sync --frozen--frozen matters — it fails the build if uv.lock is out of sync with pyproject.toml instead of silently re-resolving on CI, which is the behavior we actually wanted from Poetry the whole time and never quite trusted it to do consistently. That flag alone caught two accidental lockfile drift incidents in the first month, both cases where someone had run uv add locally, forgotten to commit the updated lock, and would previously have shipped a CI run that resolved something slightly different from what they'd tested.
What we tell teams asking whether to switch
If your monorepo’s CI install time is under a minute already, or your team runs fewer than a handful of PRs a day, the migration is mostly aesthetic — do it when you have a free afternoon, not urgently.
If you’re paying for CI minutes by the hour and your install step is a noticeable fraction of pipeline time, the math is not close. We now spend fewer CI-hours on dependency installs in a week than we used to spend in a single busy day.
Three things we’d tell ourselves before starting again:

• Generate the new lockfile fresh, then diff resolved versions against the old lock package by package — don’t skim, diff

• Run full integration tests against the new lock before merging, not just the fast unit suite

• Migrate service by service in a monorepo, not all 40 packages in one PR — isolate the blast radius of a bad resolution
Tooling migrations are rarely the interesting engineering story. This one paid for itself in the first week anyway, lockfile surprise included.
One more number worth logging: engineer time spent waiting on local pip install / poetry install during normal development, not just CI. We didn't track this rigorously before the switch, but a rough survey of the team put local dependency installs at somewhere between 5 and 15 times a week per engineer — new branch, dependency bump, clean venv after a weird local state. At even a conservative 90 seconds saved per install, across 40 engineers, that's a meaningful chunk of a workday returned every week that never shows up on a CI-hours invoice but shows up in how often someone actually reaches for uv add instead of avoiding a dependency change because installing is annoying.
If your team is juggling more than one of these — CI pain, deploy pain, the Docker build that also needs a look, the Postgres pool that’s been “fine for now” — the four-guide bundle covers the adjacent failure modes we didn’t have room for here:
→ DevOps War Room Bundle — All 4 Guides
